Mojave Nature by Kurt Buzard


Erodium cicutarium

Redstem Stork's Bill

A ubiquitous and hardy winter annual, recognized by its fern-like foliage and distinctive long, needle-like fruits that resemble a bird's beak. Erodium cicutarium is one of the first plants to bloom in the desert spring. While non-native, it has become deeply integrated into the desert ecology. Its seed dispersal mechanism is a marvel of biological engineering: when dry, the tail of the seed twists into a tight spiral, and when moistened by dew or rain, it uncoils, effectively drilling the seed into the soil.

Learn More Details About This Plant

Plant Data

Names and Taxonomy

  • Common Names: redstem stork's billfilareepin clover
  • Synonyms: geranium cicutarium
  • Family: geraniaceae
  • Genus Information: CA 8, NV 4, AZ 4, UT 4, NM 4, TX 4

Habit and Size

  • Duration: annual or biennial herb
  • Habit: prostrate to ascending
  • Shape: rosette-forming / spreading
  • Size: 5–50 cm wide (stems can reach 30 cm tall)
  • Stem: reddish, hairy, and often spreading along the ground

Leaves

  • Color: green to reddish-green
  • Shape: pinnate / fern-like with deeply lobed leaflets
  • Edge: serrate to incised
  • Arrangement: basal rosette (early), alternate (on stems)
  • Leaf Retention: annual

Flowers and Fruit

  • Sex: hermaphroditic
  • Flower Color: pink to lavender-purple
  • Flower Size: 10–15 mm diameter
  • Number of Petals: 5
  • Bloom: February–May
  • Fruit: 5-parted schizocarp with a long, spirally twisting "beak"

Habitat and Growing Conditions

  • Water Use: low to moderate
  • Light: full sun to partial shade
  • Soil: wide variety; thrives in disturbed, sandy, or loamy soils
  • Habitat: disturbed sites, desert flats, grasslands, and roadsides

Distribution

  • Distribution: widespread throughout North America (introduced from Eurasia)
  • Seen:
  • Elevation: 0–7000 feet (0–2135 meters)

Gallery

Erodium cicutarium

Noted for its aggressive early spring carpet of pink flowers.
